summary:
The Battle of Rossbach (November 5, 1757 AD) was one of [[event:2386|Frederick the Great's]] most celebrated victories — a tactical masterpiece in which a Prussian force of approximately 22,000 men destroyed a combined French and Imperial (Holy Roman Empire) army of 41,000 in a battle lasting less than 90 minutes with minimal Prussian casualties. Frederick, facing a potentially fatal encirclement by the coalition of France, Austria, Russia, and Sweden during the [[event:2332|Seven Years' War]], achieved the victory through superlative operational mobility: his army broke camp and marched across the enemy's front while they were still maneuvering to attack, then struck their flank and rear with devastating cavalry charges by General Seydlitz before deploying his infantry for the kill.

Rossbach had ramifications beyond its immediate military significance. French casualties were approximately 10,000 (killed, wounded, and captured) against Prussian losses of under 600 — a disparity that humiliated France and demonstrated that its army, considered the finest in Europe at the start of the war, had become bureaucratically sclerotic and tactically inferior. The battle accelerated French calls for military reform (fulfilled by the reforms of Saint-Germain after the Seven Years' War) and contributed to the public ridicule of French military competence that would shape attitudes toward the Army on the eve of the [[event:107|French Revolution]]. For Prussia, Rossbach — fought just five weeks before Frederick's even greater victory at Leuthen — confirmed the martial reputation that made Prussia synonymous with military excellence and provided the foundational myths that would inspire the Prussian reformers of the Napoleonic era.
